comments_and_readability.label: Comments and Readability
comments_and_readability.summary: Learn how SQL comments work, how to explain logic, how to name output clearly, and how to avoid messy SQL.

sketch2.title: How do you name output clearly?

sketch3.title: How do you avoid messy SQL?

sql_comments.title: SQL comments are notes for humans reading the query.
sql_comments.bodyMarkdown:
A **comment** is text inside the query that explains something to the reader.

Comments do not exist to change the result.
They exist to help a human understand the query.

A beginner often uses comments to explain:

- what the query is doing
- why a join is needed
- what a specific filter means

So the big idea is:

> comments help readers understand the query logic

explaining_logic.title: Explaining logic makes a query easier to review later.
explaining_logic.bodyMarkdown:
A query can be correct but still hard to understand.

A short note can help explain:

- why a table is being joined
- why a filter exists
- what the output is meant to show

So the key idea is:

> a small explanation can make the purpose of the query much clearer

naming_output_clearly.title: Readable output names help the result explain itself.
naming_output_clearly.bodyMarkdown:
Clear output labels make the result easier to interpret.

This matters even more when the query includes:

- joins
- repeated names
- calculated values

So the main idea is:

> readable output names help the result table say what each column means

avoiding_messy_sql.title: Readable SQL avoids clutter, confusion, and unclear naming.
avoiding_messy_sql.bodyMarkdown:
Messy SQL often feels hard to trust because the logic is difficult to follow.

A beginner-friendly query usually avoids:

- crowded formatting
- unclear aliases
- unlabeled output
- no explanation at all in complex spots

So the important idea is:

> readable SQL is easier to understand, review, and fix

m9_s03_query_with_comment.title: Use a SQL comment
m9_s03_query_with_comment.prompt:
The SQLite workspace already has the `school_relations_intro` dataset loaded.

Write a query that returns `students.name` and `enrollments.term` by joining `students` to `enrollments`.

Add a short SQL comment above the query explaining what the query is meant to show, then click **Run**.

help.concept: A comment can explain the purpose of the query without changing the result.
help.hint_1: Write a short note above the query, then write the join normally.
help.hint_2: Add one brief comment that explains the goal of the query, then build the join using the student relationship.

m9_s03_query_with_comment.hint: Add a brief comment above the query explaining its purpose.
m9_s03_query_with_comment.starterCode: -- Explain what this query will show

m9_s03_explain_join_logic.title: Explain join logic with a comment
m9_s03_explain_join_logic.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns `students.name` and `courses.title` by joining through `enrollments`.

Add a short comment explaining that `enrollments` is the bridge between the two tables, then run it.

help.concept: Comments can explain the logic of a relationship path that may not be obvious at first glance.
help.hint_1: Add a note explaining the bridge table, then write the two-step join normally.
help.hint_2: Use a short comment to explain why the middle table exists in the query, then build the join path through it.

m9_s03_explain_join_logic.hint: Explain the bridge table briefly, then write the join.
m9_s03_explain_join_logic.starterCode: -- Explain why the middle table is needed

m9_s03_name_output_clearly.title: Name output clearly
m9_s03_name_output_clearly.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns `students.name` and `courses.title` through `enrollments`, but rename the output columns to `student_name` and `course_title`.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: Readable output names make joined results easier to interpret.
help.hint_1: Write the join normally, then alias the selected output columns clearly.
help.hint_2: Select one column from `students` and one from `courses`, and give both result columns clearer labels.

m9_s03_name_output_clearly.hint: Use clear aliases for the output columns.
m9_s03_name_output_clearly.starterCode: -- Return a readable joined result with clear output names

m9_s03_comment_and_alias_report.title: Combine comments and readability
m9_s03_comment_and_alias_report.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns `courses.title` and `departments.name` by joining `courses` to `departments`.

Add a short comment explaining the purpose of the query, and rename the department result column to `department_name`, then run it.

help.concept: Comments explain the purpose, while aliases make the output easier to read.
help.hint_1: Add one short note, then write the join and rename the department result.
help.hint_2: Use a small comment for the reader, then combine a direct join with a clearer output label.

m9_s03_comment_and_alias_report.hint: Use one short comment and one clear result alias.

m9_s03_readability_project.title: Practice comments and readability
m9_s03_readability_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice comments and readable SQL on the `school_relations_intro` dataset.

For each task:

1. write the query clearly
2. add comments only when they help explain the purpose or logic
3. click **Run**
4. check whether the result labels are easy to understand

Tasks:

1. Add a comment above a students-and-enrollments join
2. Add a comment explaining why `enrollments` is needed between students and courses
3. Rename joined output columns clearly
4. Add a comment and clear output aliases to a courses-and-departments query
5. Write a readable query that shows `students.name` and `grade_level` with a short purpose comment

Run one query at a time.
This project is about making queries easier for a human reader to understand.

help.concept: Readable SQL combines helpful comments, clear labels, and uncluttered structure.
help.hint_1: Only use comments where they help the reader understand the goal or logic.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. Focus on whether the query would make sense to a beginner reading it later.

m9_s03_readability_project.hint: Use comments when they clarify the purpose or logic, and use aliases for clearer output.
m9_s03_readability_project.starterCode:
-- Task 1
-- Add a short comment above a students-and-enrollments join

m9_s03_sql_comments_meaning.title: What are SQL comments for?
m9_s03_sql_comments_meaning.prompt: What is the main purpose of SQL comments in beginner queries?

help.concept: Comments are notes for humans reading the query, not instructions that change the result.
help.hint_2: The point is to explain what the query is doing or why it is written that way.

options.a: To help humans understand the query
options.b: To change which rows are returned

m9_s03_explaining_logic_meaning.title: Why explain query logic?
m9_s03_explaining_logic_meaning.prompt: Why can it be helpful to explain the logic of a SQL query?

help.concept: A short explanation can make the purpose or structure of the query much easier to understand later.
help.hint_2: It helps readers understand why the query is written that way.

m9_s03_explaining_logic_meaning.hint: Think easier to review and understand later.

options.a: It makes the purpose and structure easier to understand
options.c: It removes the need for IDs
options.d: It forces the result to be sorted

m9_s03_naming_output_meaning.title: Why name output clearly?
m9_s03_naming_output_meaning.prompt: Why are clear output names useful in SQL results?

help.concept: Readable output labels help the result explain itself.
help.hint_1: Think result headers that make sense quickly.
help.hint_2: A reader should be able to look at the result and understand what each column means.

options.a: They help the result table explain what each column means
options.b: They permanently rename the stored column
options.c: They remove the need for formatting

m9_s03_avoiding_messy_sql_meaning.title: What helps avoid messy SQL?
m9_s03_avoiding_messy_sql_meaning.prompt: What is one of the best ways to avoid messy SQL?

help.concept: Messy SQL becomes easier to avoid when the query has clear formatting, naming, and helpful explanation.
help.hint_2: A query is easier to trust when the purpose and logic are easy to follow.

m9_s03_avoiding_messy_sql_meaning.hint: Think clear structure, clear labels, clear purpose.

options.a: Use clear formatting, helpful labels, and explanation when needed
options.b: Write everything on one crowded line
options.d: Hide the logic from the reader

m9_s03_when_comments_help.title: When are comments most helpful?
m9_s03_when_comments_help.prompt: When are comments especially helpful in beginner SQL?

help.concept: Comments help most when the purpose or logic might not be obvious to the reader.
help.hint_1: Think places a beginner might get confused.
help.hint_2: A brief note helps when the query structure or reason is not immediately clear.

m9_s03_when_comments_help.hint: Think places where the purpose or logic needs explanation.

options.a: When the purpose or logic may not be obvious to the reader
options.c: Only in tables with one column

m9_s03_comments_true.title: What is true about SQL comments?
m9_s03_comments_true.prompt: Select all true statements about SQL comments.

help.concept: Comments are for human readers and can explain the purpose or logic of the query.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about helping readers understand the query.

m9_s03_comments_true.hint: Think comments help humans, not the result logic.

options.a: Comments can explain what a query is doing
options.b: Comments can explain why a join is used
options.c: Comments can make review easier later
options.d: Comments automatically change the result rows
options.e: Comments are mainly for human readers

m9_s03_logic_true.title: What is true about explaining logic in SQL?
m9_s03_logic_true.prompt: Select all true statements about explaining query logic.

help.concept: Explaining logic can make a correct query much easier to understand.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about making the query easier to follow.

m9_s03_logic_true.hint: Think easier to understand why the query works that way.

options.a: A short note can explain why a bridge table is needed
options.b: A short note can explain the purpose of a query
options.c: Logic explanation can help another learner read the query later
options.d: Logic explanation means the query no longer needs correct SQL

m9_s03_output_names_true.title: What is true about naming output clearly?
m9_s03_output_names_true.prompt: Select all true statements about clear output names in SQL results.

help.concept: Clear output names improve readability of the final result table.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about readable labels and easier interpretation.

m9_s03_output_names_true.hint: Think output labels that explain themselves.

options.a: Clear output names can make results easier to interpret
options.b: Joined results often benefit from better output names
options.c: Aliases can help create clearer output names
options.d: Clear output names permanently change the schema
options.e: Readable headers make reports easier to scan

m9_s03_messy_sql_true.title: What is true about avoiding messy SQL?
m9_s03_messy_sql_true.prompt: Select all true statements about avoiding messy SQL.

help.concept: Readable SQL usually uses clear formatting, helpful naming, and explanation where needed.
help.hint_1: Think readable and easy to review.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about reducing clutter and confusion.

options.a: Readable formatting can make SQL less messy
options.c: Useful comments can clarify purpose or logic
options.d: Messy SQL is always better for beginners
options.e: Clean SQL is easier to review and fix

m9_s03_readability_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for comments and readability?
m9_s03_readability_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for writing readable SQL with comments.

help.concept: A good workflow is to write the query clearly, add comments only when they help, and make the output easy to understand.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that improve readability for a human reader.

m9_s03_readability_workflow_true.hint: Write clearly, comment where needed, and label the output well.

options.a: Write the query in a readable layout
options.b: Add comments when they help explain purpose or logic
options.d: Check whether another beginner could understand the query
options.e: Avoid readability once the query runs
